DTEQ MIRO HUB
3
Digital Techniques for Urban Designers

DTEQ MODULE 3

Instructor: Donnal Baijnauth

In this module, we will explore rendering, animation, and video production tools with a focus on visual storytelling. These techniques help transform plans, diagrams, and 3D models into compelling narratives that communicate design intent, reveal key insights, and make projects more dynamic, understandable, and grounded in their context.

DAY 01
Rendering with Lumion & Twinmotion
02 LESSONS  ·  Intro Tutorials
01 Introduction to Lumion 02 Introduction to Twinmotion
▶ FULL PLAYLIST ↓ DEMO FILES
01_01

Introduction to Lumion

1.1 — Lumion Interface, Model Setup & First Render VIDEO TUTORIAL
Step-by-Step Tutorial
01
Open Lumion and Start a New Scene
Launch Lumion and begin with a new scene or template. Choose a simple starting environment so the focus stays on learning the interface, navigation, and basic scene setup before adding detailed assets.
[ Screenshot: Lumion start screen / new scene template selection ]
02
Import the 3D Model
Use the Import tool to bring in a model from your design software. After importing, place the model on the terrain and check that the scale, orientation, and ground elevation look correct.
[ Screenshot: Import model panel with imported file selected ]
03
Navigate Around the Scene
Practice orbiting, panning, and zooming around the model. Get comfortable moving between aerial views, street-level views, and close-up detail views so you can quickly compose render scenes.
[ Screenshot: Model placed in Lumion scene with navigation view active ]
04
Apply and Adjust Materials
Select surfaces and assign Lumion materials such as glass, concrete, asphalt, planting, or metal. Adjust material settings like scale, reflectivity, roughness, and color so the model begins to read as a finished environment.
[ Screenshot: Material library / material settings panel ]
05
Add Context, Landscape, and Entourage
Use Lumion’s object library to add trees, people, vehicles, furniture, and site elements. Place these strategically to show scale, activity, and the intended atmosphere of the urban scene.
[ Screenshot: Object library with trees / people added to scene ]
06
Set Up Camera Views and Render Output
Move into Photo mode, choose a camera angle, and save the view. Add basic effects like real sky, shadows, reflection, color correction, and exposure. Export a still image once the view feels clear and presentation-ready.
[ Screenshot: Photo mode with effects stack and render button ]
// NOTE

Screenshot pass: After confirming the exact video flow, replace each placeholder with a still from the corresponding moment in the tutorial. The goal is for students to be able to follow the page without constantly rewinding the video.

01_02

Introduction to Twinmotion

1.2 — Twinmotion Interface, Import Workflow & Media Export VIDEO TUTORIAL
Step-by-Step Tutorial
01
Open Twinmotion and Start a New Project
Launch Twinmotion and begin with a new project file. Take a moment to identify the main workspace areas: viewport, scene graph, library, import panel, and media/export tools.
[ Screenshot: Twinmotion opening workspace / interface overview ]
02
Import or Direct Link a Design Model
Bring in a model using Import or Direct Link, depending on the source software. Confirm that geometry comes in at the correct size and that the model is positioned correctly in the scene.
[ Screenshot: Import / Direct Link panel with model loaded ]
03
Assign Materials from the Twinmotion Library
Drag materials from the library onto the model. Adjust scale, color, reflectivity, and surface qualities so exterior paving, facade materials, landscape, and glazing feel intentional.
[ Screenshot: Material library with material applied to model ]
04
Add People, Vegetation, and Site Assets
Use the asset library to populate the scene with trees, people, cars, seating, lighting, and other urban elements. Keep placement purposeful so the entourage supports the design story rather than cluttering the view.
[ Screenshot: Scene with vegetation / people / street assets added ]
05
Set Lighting, Weather, and Time of Day
Adjust the sun position, sky, weather, and season settings to create a clear visual mood. Use lighting to emphasize building form, public space, and key circulation areas.
[ Screenshot: Environment settings with sun / weather controls ]
06
Create Media Views and Export
Move into the Media tools to save still images, panoramas, or video paths. Compose views with a clear subject, save each camera, and export high-resolution files for presentation or post-production.
[ Screenshot: Media export panel with image / video options ]
// NOTE

For this module: Lumion and Twinmotion should be framed as parallel rendering workflows. Students do not need to master both immediately, but they should understand how each tool imports models, builds scenes, adds realism, and exports presentation media.

01 Introduction to After Effects 02 Layers & Trim Paths 03 Transform Animation 04 PreComps & Export
▶ FULL PLAYLIST ↓ DEMO FILES
02_01

Introduction to After Effects

2.1 — Interface, Project Setup & Basic Workflow VIDEO TUTORIAL
Step-by-Step Tutorial
01
Open After Effects and Create a New Project
Launch After Effects and start a new project. Save the file into a dedicated course folder so the project, assets, and exports stay organized from the beginning.
[ Screenshot: After Effects start screen / new project ]
02
Create a New Composition
Create a new composition for the animation. Use standard presentation settings such as 1920 × 1080, 30 frames per second, and a short working duration. Name the composition clearly so it is easy to find later.
[ Screenshot: Composition Settings dialog ]
03
Review the Main Interface Areas
Identify the Project panel for files, the Composition viewer for previews, the Timeline for layers and keyframes, and the Properties or Effects panels for adjustments.
[ Screenshot: After Effects interface labeled by panel ]
04
Import Course Assets
Import image, Illustrator, or video assets into the Project panel. Keep files organized in folders such as Images, Illustrator, Comps, and Exports.
[ Screenshot: Project panel with organized folders ]
05
Place Assets into the Timeline
Drag assets into the composition. Each asset becomes its own layer in the timeline. Reorder layers to control what appears in front, behind, or underneath other elements.
[ Screenshot: Timeline with multiple layers stacked ]
06
Preview the Animation
Use the spacebar or Preview panel to play the composition. This first lesson is about getting comfortable moving between the project panel, composition viewer, and timeline before creating more advanced motion.
02_02

Organizing Layers, Importing from Illustrator & Trim Paths

2.2 — Illustrator Import, Layer Structure & Draw-On Linework VIDEO TUTORIAL
Step-by-Step Tutorial
01
Prepare the Illustrator File
In Illustrator, organize the artwork into clear layers before importing. Separate base maps, labels, paths, fills, and highlight elements so they can be animated independently.
[ Screenshot: Illustrator layers panel with named layers ]
02
Import as a Composition
In After Effects, import the Illustrator file as a Composition instead of flat footage. This preserves the layer structure and creates a composition with each Illustrator layer available for animation.
[ Screenshot: Import dialog set to Composition / Retain Layer Sizes ]
03
Organize and Rename Layers
Open the imported composition and check the timeline. Rename layers as needed, group related items visually, and remove anything unnecessary.
[ Screenshot: Organized AE timeline with renamed Illustrator layers ]
04
Convert Linework to Shape Layers
Select the Illustrator line layer and create shape layers from the vector artwork. Shape layers allow After Effects to access path animation tools such as Trim Paths.
[ Screenshot: Create Shapes from Vector Layer command ]
05
Add Trim Paths
Open the shape layer properties, go to Contents, and add Trim Paths. Animate the End value from 0% to 100% so the line draws itself onto the map or diagram over time.
[ Screenshot: Trim Paths End property with keyframes ]
06
Stagger Multiple Animated Lines
If the graphic has multiple lines or paths, offset the keyframes so each one draws in sequence. This creates a clearer visual narrative and helps direct attention through the diagram.
[ Screenshot: Timeline with staggered Trim Path keyframes ]
// TIP

For urban diagrams: Trim Paths works especially well for transit routes, circulation arrows, site boundaries, movement paths, and phased diagram overlays.

02_03

Opacity, Position, Rotation & Anchor Point

2.3 — Transform Properties & Keyframe Motion VIDEO TUTORIAL
Step-by-Step Tutorial
01
Select a Layer and Reveal Transform Properties
Select a layer in the timeline and open its Transform properties. The key properties for this lesson are Opacity, Position, Rotation, and Anchor Point.
[ Screenshot: Transform properties expanded in timeline ]
02
Animate Opacity
Use Opacity to fade graphics in or out. Set the layer to 0%, create a keyframe, move forward in time, and set it to 100%.
[ Screenshot: Opacity keyframes from 0% to 100% ]
03
Animate Position
Use Position to move a layer across the composition. Set a starting keyframe, move the playhead, then reposition the layer. After Effects creates the motion between the two points.
[ Screenshot: Position path visible in composition viewer ]
04
Animate Rotation
Use Rotation for arrows, compass elements, icons, or graphic callouts. Add rotation keyframes to spin or pivot the object. Keep rotation subtle unless it serves a clear instructional purpose.
[ Screenshot: Rotation property with keyframes ]
05
Adjust the Anchor Point
The Anchor Point controls where a layer scales or rotates from. Move it to the center, edge, or base of an object depending on the desired motion.
[ Screenshot: Anchor Point tool positioning pivot on graphic ]
06
Apply Easing for Smoother Motion
Select keyframes and apply Easy Ease to soften the movement. Eased keyframes feel more intentional and polished than linear motion.
[ Screenshot: Easy Ease keyframes / graph editor curve ]
// TIP

Shortcut keys: T reveals Opacity, P reveals Position, R reveals Rotation, and A reveals Anchor Point. These shortcuts make the timeline much faster to navigate.

02_04

PreComp, Layer Mask & Exporting

2.4 — PreComposing, Masking & Final Output VIDEO TUTORIAL
Step-by-Step Tutorial
01
Select Layers to PreCompose
Select related layers in the timeline, such as a diagram, label group, or animated graphic sequence. PreComposing packages those layers into a separate composition.
[ Screenshot: Multiple layers selected in timeline ]
02
Create the PreComp
Use the PreCompose command and give the new comp a clear name. Move all attributes into the new composition when you want the internal animation to stay contained inside the PreComp.
[ Screenshot: PreCompose dialog with named composition ]
03
Use a Layer Mask
Select a layer and draw a mask with the shape or pen tool. Masks control which parts of the layer are visible and are useful for cropping, reveals, or hiding parts of a composition.
[ Screenshot: Mask drawn over composition layer ]
04
Animate Mask Path or Feather
Open the mask properties and adjust Mask Path, Mask Feather, Mask Opacity, or Mask Expansion. These settings can create soft reveals, wipes, and controlled transitions.
[ Screenshot: Mask properties expanded with keyframes ]
05
Check the Final Composition
Return to the main composition, preview the full animation, and check timing, visibility, and layer order. Make sure the animation communicates clearly before exporting.
[ Screenshot: Main composition preview before export ]
06
Export the Animation
Send the composition to the Render Queue or Adobe Media Encoder. Export a high-quality file for editing and a compressed MP4 for easy sharing.
[ Screenshot: Export settings / Media Encoder queue ]
// EXPORT

Recommended workflow: Keep a high-quality master export for editing and create a separate compressed MP4 for class upload, presentation playback, or web use.

01 Introduction to Premiere Pro
▶ FULL PLAYLIST ↓ DEMO FILES
03_01

Introduction to Premiere Pro

3.1 — Premiere Pro Interface, Timeline Setup & Final Export VIDEO TUTORIAL
Step-by-Step Tutorial
01
Create a New Premiere Pro Project
Open Premiere Pro and create a new project. Name the file clearly and save it into the same organized folder structure as your renders, animations, and audio assets.
[ Screenshot: Premiere Pro new project window ]
02
Import Media Assets
Import video clips, After Effects exports, rendered stills, audio files, and any graphics you plan to use. Keep assets organized in bins such as Video, Motion Graphics, Audio, Images, and Exports.
[ Screenshot: Project panel with organized bins ]
03
Create a Sequence
Create a new sequence that matches your intended output format. For most class presentations, use 1920 × 1080 with a standard frame rate such as 30fps, unless your source footage requires something different.
[ Screenshot: Sequence settings dialog ]
04
Place Clips on the Timeline
Drag media into the timeline in the order of your story. Start with context, move into process or analysis, then end with the final output. The timeline is where the full video narrative is assembled.
[ Screenshot: Premiere timeline with clips arranged ]
05
Trim and Rearrange Clips
Use the Selection tool and Razor tool to trim clips, remove unnecessary portions, and adjust pacing. Keep edits clean and purposeful so the video supports the design narrative instead of feeling overly edited.
[ Screenshot: Razor tool / trimmed timeline clips ]
06
Add Titles and Graphics
Use titles for project names, section breaks, captions, or location labels. Keep text simple, consistent, and legible. Avoid overusing animated title effects unless they support the presentation style.
[ Screenshot: Essential Graphics panel with title layer ]
07
Add Audio and Adjust Levels
Add voiceover, music, or ambient audio if needed. Adjust audio levels so speech is clear and background music stays secondary. Use fades at the beginning and end of audio clips for smoother transitions.
[ Screenshot: Audio track levels and fade handles ]
08
Review Playback and Timing
Play the sequence from beginning to end. Check whether the pacing feels clear, whether any clip is too long, and whether the order of information helps the viewer understand the project.
09
Export the Final Video
Go to Export and choose H.264 for a shareable MP4. Use Match Source - High Bitrate as a starting point, confirm the output name and destination, then export the final video.
[ Screenshot: Export settings set to H.264 / Match Source High Bitrate ]
// EXPORT

Recommended settings: Format: H.264, Preset: Match Source - High Bitrate, Resolution: 1920 × 1080, Frame Rate: match sequence, Output: MP4.

// SHORTCUTS

Useful shortcuts: V = Selection Tool, C = Razor Tool, Spacebar = Play/Pause, Ctrl/Cmd + K = Cut Clip, +/- = Timeline Zoom.
